focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 17

The rental sedans were back.

Nick spotted them from the parking lot — three silver Camrys nosed into visitor spots near the entrance, the same generic fleet that had materialized during the Manila audit in January. He sat in his Civic with the engine off and watched a woman in a gray blazer carry a banker's box of file folders through the front door. It was HR, and the plates were from San Francisco. The March sun came in low through the windshield and warmed the dashboard dust, and outside the business park the hills had turned the vivid unstable green they held for only a few weeks each spring — a saturation that would flag as unrealistic in a generated image.

He knew what the sedans meant. He'd known since the quality metrics cratered, since the automated flags went from yellow to red across the calibration pipeline, since Marcus's Slack messages shifted from encouraging to clipped. The question had never been whether but when.

The car door stuck on the first try. He shouldered it open and crossed the lot. The morning air was warm for March — Bay Area false spring, the kind that could go gray by afternoon — and the landscaping island between the parking rows had produced a single anemic daffodil from a crack in the mulch border. He walked past it and through the front door and up the stairs to the evaluation floor.

The building was already more awake than it should have been at seven-twenty. The coffee-shop chain across Davis Street was running its drive-through line, and through the second-floor windows the conference room lights were on behind drawn blinds. Someone from HR had gotten here before anyone on the floor.

Priya was already at her desk. It wasn't yet seven-thirty and she was there, her monitor showing email instead of the evaluation por...
open_hooks_to_carry: The corrupted models producing six-fingered hands, melted faces, impossible anatomy
The public response: not horror but delight — memes, merchandise, a Brooklyn art collective, think pieces about 'digital grotesque'
Tommy texting the group chat that the glitched images 'go harder than anything I saw in art school'
Nick alone in his apartment, surrounded by unsold paintings nobody rated higher than a 4, laughing until he stops

payoffs_to_land.description: Tommy's comfort with AI aesthetics (seeded in Ch 7) makes his embrace of the viral glitch outputs feel character-consistent, not plot-convenient

prev_tail_excerpt:
...in the right lane and the displaced air rocked the Civic and blew grit against his face and into the mess on the ground. He spat. Wiped his mouth with the back of his hand. His eyes watered from the retching. That was all.

Cars passed. The freeway noise was continuous, not separate vehicles but one metal roar with no seam in it. Nick sat inside it with his seatbelt still on and his hands in his lap and bile on the back of his tongue and the sour smell of his own vomit leaking through the cracked door seal.

He pulled back onto the freeway after three minutes and drove the rest of the way north at forty-five in the right lane, both hands on the wheel, the engine complaining at a pitch it did not like. Cars stacked behind him and broke around him. Several people honked. One driver held the horn for a full three seconds passing him, the sound stretching away behind the car like a wire pulled taut.

He found street parking outside his building in Fruitvale. The space went illegal after 6 PM but it was 4:20 and moving the car later would require a future version of himself to exist. The ticket would be sixty-eight dollars. He already knew the amount before he left the ignition.

The apartment was dark. He left the lights off, took off his shoes, and lay on top of the covers in his jeans and flannel.

He had expected something larger, some clean sensation he could name and then endure. Guilt. Relief. Triumph. Fear. Instead the feeling was the size of a missed bus, stupid and complete.

The sketchbook stayed in the drawer. He did not take it out to record the last day. He did not get up to eat. He did not move the car before six, and in the morning a sixty-eight-dollar ticket waited under the wiper. He folded it in half and put it in the glove compartment with the other one.
state_deltas_required: Nick's sabotage produces viral content instead of discrediting AI — his revenge is absorbed and aestheticized by the culture
Nick's revenge produces the opposite of justice; the grotesqueries become a cultural phenomenon more famous than his paintings ever were
